title: Clasped weft on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: Joining two weft colours mid-row to produce colour-block patterns without cutting yarn
excerpt: The clasped-weft technique passes two separate weft yarns from opposite sides of the shed, clasping them together where they meet in the shed. By varying where in the weft the two yarns clasp, you can produce colour blocks, diagonal lines, and curves on the surface of a rigid-heddle woven cloth without cutting the weft between colour sections.
sourceNotes: Clasped weft is documented in contemporary rigid-heddle weaving instruction. Method is observation-led practice consistent with guild workshop teaching.

content.text: method requires two weft yarn sources and a single open shed. Weft A enters the shed from the left and is drawn across to the target

. Weft B enters from the right and is hooked around weft A at the clasp point, then pulled back out. Both wefts are beaten and the shed changes. The clasp point is moved on each row to build a colour shape.

items.name: Rigid heddle loom, warped to desired width
Weft colour A (wound on a shuttle)
Weft colour B (wound on a separate shuttle)

content.text: Step 1: Pass weft A into the shed

Open the shed (heddle up or down). Hold shuttle A in the left hand and pass it into the shed from the left, pushing it across to the target clasp point (for example, the midpoint of the warp width). Do not pass it all the way through. Leave weft A inside the shed at the target position.
Step 2: Hook weft B around weft A

Hold shuttle B in the right hand. Loop weft B around the end of weft A inside the shed (hook one around the other). Pull shuttle B back out of the shed from the right, drawing weft B back through to the right selvedge. Both wefts are now inside the shed, meeting at the clasp point.
Step 3: Beat and change the shed

Beat the weft into place with the heddle. Change the shed (heddle down). Pass weft A from left toward the clasp point and weft B from right, repeating the clasping. On the next row, move the clasp point one or two warp threads to the left or right to build a diagonal or curved colour boundary.

The clasp loop is a small doubling of weft at the join point. Keep it at the correct tension: too loose and it forms a bump on the surface; too tight and it pulls the selvedge inward. After beating, adjust the clasp loop with a fingertip to seat it flat against the adjacent weft.

items.fix: After each pick, reach into the shed at the clasp point with a fingertip and ease the loop to the same tension as the surrounding weft.
items.cause: The clasp loop was pulled too tight or left too loose
items.problem: A bump appears at the clasp point on every row

items.fix: Mark the target clasp-point warp thread with a small piece of contrasting yarn tied loosely around it, and move the marker as you build the shape.
items.cause: Clasp point moved inconsistently between rows
items.problem: The two colour areas bleed into each other rather than having a clean boundary

items.fix: Leave a small loop at the selvedge on the right side before beating. The loop evens out during the next pass.
items.cause: Weft B was pulled back out too tightly when the shuttle exited
items.problem: The selvedge on the colour-B side pulls in
